section.title: The Purpose of Proverbs
section.sectionRationale: The book opens with a formal title, naming Solomon as its source, then states its own goals in a short prologue before landing on its motto in verse 7. Everything that follows in the extended discourses of chapters 1 to 9 and the sentence proverbs after them unpacks this opening statement.
section.thesis: Proverbs exists to train people in wisdom, discipline, and righteous living, and it plants its flag from the first page: all such wisdom begins with the fear of the LORD, not with cleverness alone.
section.frameNote: Two short movements: the title and stated purposes of the book, verses 1 to 6, and the book's motto, verse 7, which fools reject and the wise embrace.

verses.text: These are the proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel,
for gaining {{chokmah|wisdom}} and {{musar|discipline}}, for comprehending words of insight,
and for receiving instruction in wise living and in righteousness, justice, and equity.
To impart prudence to the simple and knowledge and discretion to the young,
let the wise listen and gain instruction, and the discerning acquire wise counsel
by understanding the {{mashal|proverbs}} and parables, the sayings and riddles of the wise.
The {{yirah|fear of the LORD}} is the beginning of knowledge, but fools despise wisdom and discipline.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, the practical skill of living well and rightly before God. It is not mere information but a trained ability to see life truly and respond to it rightly, the master theme of the whole book.

musar.gloss: Discipline, instruction, or correction, often the kind a parent gives a child. It can mean verbal teaching or the corrective experience of consequences, and Proverbs treats both as gifts, not punishments to be resented.

mashal.gloss: A proverb, parable, or comparison, a short saying that captures a truth by likeness or contrast so it can be remembered and applied. The book takes its Hebrew name, Mishley, from this word.

yirah.gloss: Fear, in the sense of reverent awe, humble trust, and glad submission before the LORD. It is not cringing dread but the posture of a creature who knows its Maker is holy, good, and to be taken seriously in every choice.

basic.context: Proverbs opens like a book with a title page. It names Solomon as the source of these sayings and then states, in a short list, exactly what the book is for: to teach wisdom, discipline, righteous living, and shrewd discernment, to the simple and the wise alike. Then, before a single proverb is given, the book states its foundation in one sentence, the fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a prologue, so it functions like a table of contents and a thesis statement together. The listed purposes in verses 2 to 6 describe what wisdom does; verse 7 states where wisdom starts. Read together, they teach that skill for living cannot be separated from a right relationship with God, a claim the rest of the book keeps testing and confirming.
basic.originalLanguages: Chokmah, wisdom, and musar, discipline, are paired from the first verse, showing that true skill for living is inseparable from correction humbly received. The book gets its Hebrew title, Mishley, from mashal, proverb, a short saying built on comparison. And the motto word, yirah, fear, names not terror but reverent trust, the right posture of a creature before its Maker.
basic.typology: The fear of the LORD as the beginning of wisdom finds its perfect human expression in Jesus, on whom the Spirit of wisdom and of the fear of the LORD rested, Isaiah 11:2 to 3. He is also, according to Paul, the wisdom of God in person, so the book's opening motto ultimately points to Him.
basic.christ: Proverbs 1:7 makes a claim that the rest of Scripture deepens rather than abandons: real wisdom cannot be had apart from a right relationship to God. The New Testament answers that claim in a person. Christ is made unto us wisdom from God, 1 Corinthians 1:30, and in Him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3. To fear the LORD, then, is finally to trust and follow Jesus, in whom the fear of the LORD and the wisdom of God are joined.
basic.god: God is the source of true wisdom, and He offers it freely through this book to the simple, the young, and the already wise alike. He is not distant from practical life, righteousness, justice, and equity are His concerns, and knowing Him rightly, fearing Him, is the doorway into knowing anything else rightly.
basic.crossReferences: Job 28:28 and Psalm 111:10 state the same motto in other wisdom books. Isaiah 11:2 to 3 rests this fear on the coming Messiah. James 1:5 promises wisdom to those who ask God for it. Colossians 2:3 and 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30 present Christ as the wisdom of God.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's wisdom literature begins here, with a claim that will run through the whole book, and indeed the whole Bible: knowing how to live starts with rightly fearing the LORD. That thread runs forward until it reaches Christ, the wisdom of God made flesh, in whom the fear of the LORD and the knowledge of God are fully and finally revealed.

context.literary: A formal prologue in two parts, a title identifying the author and audience, verse 1, followed by a purpose statement in an extended sentence, verses 2 to 6, and capped by the book's governing motto, verse 7. This same motto reappears at the hinge of the sentence proverbs in 9:10, framing the discourses of chapters 1 to 9.
context.historical: Solomon, famed for wisdom given by God, 1 Kings 3, stands behind the book's early chapters, though later collections name other wise men and Solomon's successors, showing the book to be a curated anthology of Israel's wisdom tradition compiled over time.
context.canonical: Proverbs sits within the wisdom literature alongside Job and Ecclesiastes. Where Job wrestles with wisdom's limits and Ecclesiastes presses its boundaries, Proverbs states the ordinary path of wisdom plainly, and its motto, the fear of the LORD, becomes a refrain across the wisdom books and the Psalms.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce, before any specific proverb is given, both what wisdom accomplishes in a life, verses 2 to 6, and where it begins, verse 7, so that every proverb that follows is read as flowing from a right relationship with God rather than as mere worldly cleverness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This opening is prescriptive by design, it states the book's purpose and demands a response, fear the LORD or remain a fool. But the individual proverbs that follow are, in the main, general observations about how life under God usually goes, not ironclad promises. That distinction, sharpened later in the book, is already implied here, since verse 7 speaks of two paths and their general outcomes, not a guarantee for every single case.

debates.question: Is the fear of the LORD in verse 7 servile dread or something else?

views.label: Reverent awe and trust
views.case: Across Proverbs, those who fear the LORD are also said to trust Him, Proverbs 3:5, and to hate evil, Proverbs 8:13, showing the fear in view is a relational reverence, not terror of an arbitrary God.

views.label: A sober awareness of God as Judge
views.case: The same fear is set against the certain judgment on fools, verses 26 to 27, so it retains a genuine weight of accountability, not merely warm feeling.

debates.bearing: Both elements belong together, the fear of the LORD is reverent trust that takes God's holiness and judgment with full seriousness; neither warmth without weight nor weight without warmth captures it alone.

study.originalLanguages: The book announces its aim with two paired words, chokmah, wisdom, and musar, discipline, held together from the very first purpose clause, since wisdom in this book is always a disciplined, correctable wisdom. Its very title, Mishley, comes from mashal, a proverb built on comparison. And its governing word, yirah, fear, names the reverent trust that verse 7 sets as the one path into everything the book offers; those who reject it are simply named fools.

typology.type: The fear of the LORD as the beginning of wisdom, Proverbs 1:7
typology.fulfillment: Christ, on whom rested the Spirit of wisdom and of the fear of the LORD, Isaiah 11:2 to 3, the one perfect wise and God fearing man.

study.christ: The book's opening claim, that wisdom begins with fearing the LORD, is not abandoned in the New Testament but fulfilled in a person. Isaiah promised a coming shoot from Jesse on whom the Spirit of wisdom and of the fear of the LORD would rest, Isaiah 11:2 to 3, and the church came to see this fulfilled in Jesus. Paul goes further still, calling Christ Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3. So the motto of Proverbs, far from being surpassed by the gospel, is answered by it, true wisdom is found in knowing and fearing God as He is revealed in His Son.
study.god: God is the giver of wisdom, offering it through this book to the naive, the young, and the discerning without partiality. He is also the one whose fear, whose reverent, trusting acknowledgment, forms the non negotiable starting point of all real understanding; to bypass Him in the name of cleverness is, in the book's own terms, simply foolishness.

crossReferences.note: Behold, the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom, and to depart from evil is understanding, the same motto from another wisdom book.
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, echoing Proverbs almost word for word.
If anyone lacks wisdom, he should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault.
In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.

study.oneStory: Proverbs opens by naming its whole purpose in a few lines and then planting its flag, wisdom starts with fearing the LORD. That single claim shapes every proverb that follows, and it carries forward through the wisdom literature until it meets its fullest answer in Christ, the wisdom of God, in whom the fear of the LORD is perfectly realized and freely given to all who trust Him.

sermon.title: The Beginning of Wisdom
sermon.why: Begg works directly through Proverbs 1:7, explaining the fear of the LORD as humility, trust, confidence, and obedience grounded in the love shown in Christ, and identifying Jesus as the wisdom and righteousness of God in whom true wisdom is found.

quiz.q: According to verses 2 to 6, what is Proverbs meant to accomplish?

options.text: To teach wisdom, discipline, righteous living, and discernment to the simple and the wise alike.
options.note: This is exactly the stated purpose of the book in its opening sentence.

options.text: To record the political history of Solomon's reign.
options.note: Proverbs is wisdom literature, not a royal history.

options.text: To provide legal rulings for Israel's courts.
options.note: That role belongs to the Law, not to this book of proverbs.

options.text: To entertain readers with clever riddles only.
options.note: Riddles and sayings are mentioned, verse 6, but the deeper aim is moral and practical wisdom, not entertainment.

quiz.q: What is the book's motto, stated in verse 7?

options.text: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge.
options.note: This is the exact governing statement of the book.

options.text: Wisdom is available to anyone who works hard enough.
options.note: Effort matters in Proverbs, but the starting point named here is the fear of the LORD, not sheer effort.

options.text: Kings alone can attain true wisdom.
options.note: The book addresses the simple and the young as much as the wise, not kings alone.

options.text: Knowledge and the fear of the LORD are separate paths.
options.note: Close in naming both terms, but the verse ties them together as one path, not two separate ones.

quiz.q: What does it mean that fools 'despise wisdom and discipline', verse 7?

options.text: They reject correction and reverence for God, choosing self reliance instead.
options.note: This is the contrast the verse draws with those who fear the LORD.

options.text: They simply lack information or schooling.
options.note: Biblical folly is a moral and relational stance, not mere ignorance.

options.text: They are people God has predestined never to learn.
options.note: The book repeatedly calls fools to turn and listen, assuming a real choice is possible.

options.text: They are people who have not yet had the chance to hear wisdom.
options.note: Some fools may indeed be untaught, but the verse's own emphasis is on despising, an active rejection, not mere lack of exposure.

quiz.q: How does the book intend its proverbs, mashal, to function, per verse 6?

options.text: As memorable comparisons and sayings that train discernment when rightly understood.
options.note: Verse 6 speaks of understanding proverbs, parables, sayings, and riddles, tools for training discernment.

options.text: As binding legal statutes with fixed penalties.
options.note: Proverbs are wisdom sayings, not law code statutes.

options.text: As poetic decoration with no practical use.
options.note: The stated purpose is training in wise living, not decoration.

options.text: As predictions that always come true without exception.
options.note: This overstates the genre; proverbs generally describe how life under God tends to go, not ironclad predictions in every case.

quiz.q: How does Christ relate to the book's opening motto?

options.text: He is the wisdom of God in person, the one on whom the Spirit of wisdom and the fear of the LORD perfectly rested.
options.note: Isaiah 11:2 to 3 and 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30 draw this connection directly.

options.text: He has no connection to Old Testament wisdom themes.
options.note: The New Testament explicitly identifies Christ with wisdom, contradicting this.

options.text: He replaces the fear of the LORD with a purely intellectual faith.
options.note: This misreads both fear and faith; in Christ, reverent trust is deepened, not replaced with mere intellectual assent.

options.text: He is only an example of a wise teacher, like Solomon.
options.note: Correct as far as it goes, but understates the New Testament's stronger claim that Christ Himself is the wisdom of God, not merely a wise teacher.

questions: The book states its purpose before giving a single proverb. What are you hoping wisdom will do for your life right now, and does that match what verses 2 to 6 promise?
Verse 7 says wisdom begins with fearing the LORD, not with cleverness alone. Where are you tempted to pursue skill, success, or knowledge while sidelining your relationship with God?
Fools, in this verse, are those who despise discipline. How do you tend to respond when you are corrected, whether by Scripture, by a friend, or by circumstances?
The fear of the LORD is reverent trust, not cringing dread. How would your daily decisions change if you approached God with that kind of confident reverence?
Christ is called the wisdom of God. What would it look like this week to seek wisdom for a real decision by turning first to Him rather than only to your own reasoning?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew word rendered simple in Proverbs refers to one who is naive, without moral direction, or inclined toward evil, not merely young or inexperienced.
Riddles renders a Hebrew term for enigmatic or twisted sayings, showing that Proverbs expects readers to think hard, not just receive information passively.
The Hebrew words rendered fool throughout Proverbs denote one who is morally deficient, not simply unintelligent; the term carries an ethical and spiritual weight from its first use here.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 1:1-7. No em or en dashes present in this passage; none used anywhere in the file.
